title: Class vs Instance Variables
subtitle: Understand what is shared vs what belongs to each object
learning_objectives: Distinguish between class variables (shared) and instance variables (per object)
Use a class variable as a counter across all instances
Know when to use each type of variable

sections.heading: Class Variables vs Instance Variables
sections.content:
Instance variables (set with self.x = ...) belong to one specific object — each object has its own copy.

Class variables are defined directly in the class body (not in a method). They are shared by all instances of that class.
sections.code_example:
class Dog:
    species = "Canis lupus familiaris"  # class variable

    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name  # instance variable

rex = Dog("Rex")
bella = Dog("Bella")

# Both share the same species
print(rex.species)    # Canis lupus familiaris
print(bella.species)  # Canis lupus familiaris

# But each has their own name
print(rex.name)    # Rex
print(bella.name)  # Bella
sections.code_output:
Canis lupus familiaris
Canis lupus familiaris
Rex
Bella

sections.heading: Using a Class Variable as a Counter
sections.content: A common use of class variables is to count how many objects have been created.
sections.code_example:
class Car:
    count = 0  # shared by all Car objects

    def __init__(self, make):
        self.make = make
        Car.count += 1  # increment the shared counter

c1 = Car("Toyota")
c2 = Car("Honda")
c3 = Car("BMW")

print("Cars created:", Car.count)

sections.heading: @classmethod and @staticmethod
sections.content: @classmethod methods receive the class (cls) instead of an instance. @staticmethod methods receive neither — they are just regular functions placed inside the class for organisational purposes.

exercises.title: Count Instances
exercises.prompt: Define a Student class with a class variable 'count = 0'. In __init__, take a name parameter and increment Student.count. Create 3 Student objects then print Student.count.
exercises.starter_code:
class Student:
    count = 0

    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        # Increment Student.count here

s1 = Student("Alice")
s2 = Student("Bob")
s3 = Student("Carol")
print(Student.count)
exercises.solution:
class Student:
    count = 0

    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        Student.count += 1

s1 = Student("Alice")
s2 = Student("Bob")
s3 = Student("Carol")
print(Student.count)
hints: Use the class name (Student.count), not self.count, to modify the class variable
After 3 students are created, Student.count should be 3

exercises.title: Static Method
exercises.prompt: Add a @staticmethod called is_valid_score(score) to the Grader class that returns True if score is between 0 and 100 (inclusive), otherwise False. Print the results of is_valid_score(85) and is_valid_score(110).
hints: Python allows chained comparisons like 0 <= score <= 100
No self or cls needed — it is a @staticmethod

quiz.question: What is the difference between a class variable and an instance variable?
options: Class variables are shared by all instances; instance variables belong to one specific object
Class variables can only hold numbers
quiz.explanation: A class variable is defined in the class body and is shared by all objects created from that class. An instance variable is set with self.x = ... inside a method and belongs only to that specific object.

quiz.question: How do you access a class variable called 'count' from inside an instance method?
quiz.explanation: Use ClassName.count (e.g. Car.count). While you can also use self.count to read it, using the class name makes it clear you are modifying the shared class variable, not creating a new instance variable.

completion_message: You have completed the OOP module! You now understand classes, objects, inheritance, and more — that is a huge milestone!
